skill.name: qiskit
skill.description: Guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through Qiskit workflows for quantum circuits, simulators, hardware backends, transpilation, visualization, and algorithms.
skill.summary: Build, optimize, run, and explain quantum computing workflows with Qiskit.
skill.author: davila7

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many markdown code fences, quantum terminology, documentation URLs, and file-output examples as security issues. Manual review found no malicious intent, no prompt-injection text, and no executable skill code that performs command execution, exfiltration, or unauthorized system access. Residual risk is low because users may install packages, configure IBM Quantum credentials, call cloud backends, or save local output files when following examples.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Command Examples
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The external command detections are fenced documentation examples for package installation, virtual environments, and Qiskit usage. They are not executed by the skill and do not interpolate user input. Confidence: 0.94. Confidence reasoning: The reviewed locations are markdown code fences and explanatory examples; no runnable hook, script, or command wrapper was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed locations are markdown code fences and explanatory examples. No runnable hook, script, or command wrapper was found.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Quantum Terms Matched Security Keywords
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. C2 and weak-cryptography detections map to quantum-computing words such as controlled gates, backends, primitives, Hamiltonians, QFT, and algorithm names. No evidence found of command-and-control behavior or cryptographic downgrade logic. Confidence: 0.92. Confidence reasoning: The flagged lines are ordinary Qiskit concepts in tutorial prose and Python examples, not malware control channels or crypto implementations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged lines are ordinary Qiskit concepts in tutorial prose and Python examples. They do not implement malware control channels or cryptographic downgrade logic.

low_findings.title: Low Risk: User-Initiated Credentials, Network, and File Output
low_findings.description: Verdict: TRUE_POSITIVE_LOW_IMPACT. The skill documents IBM Quantum token setup, cloud backend execution, documentation links, and saving result files. These are legitimate Qiskit workflows, but users should avoid pasting real tokens into shared prompts and should review file paths before writing outputs. Confidence: 0.86. Confidence reasoning: The behaviors are clearly documented user workflows with placeholder tokens and local file names, but they still touch credentials, network services, and the filesystem.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The behaviors are clearly documented user workflows with placeholder tokens and local file names. They still involve credentials, network services, and filesystem writes.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Documentation URLs
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. Hardcoded URLs point to official Qiskit, IBM Quantum, PyPI, and ecosystem documentation. No evidence found that the skill sends user data to those URLs. Confidence: 0.95. Confidence reasoning: The URLs appear as resource links or account setup references, not as network request code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs appear as resource links or account setup references. They are not network request code and do not exfiltrate data.

content.value_statement: Quantum projects require precise circuit, backend, and algorithm choices. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code practical Qiskit guidance.
actual_capabilities: Explains how to create Qiskit circuits with gates, measurements, barriers, composition, and parameters.
Guides local execution with StatevectorSampler and StatevectorEstimator primitives.
Shows how to transpile circuits for hardware backends and choose optimization levels.
Covers IBM Quantum Runtime workflows, sessions, batch jobs, backend status, and job retrieval.
Provides examples for VQE, QAOA, Grover search, quantum kernels, chemistry, and benchmarking.
Documents visualization options for circuits, histograms, state plots, and saved figures.
limitations: It is reference material, not a verified Qiskit package or runtime environment.
Examples may need updates when Qiskit APIs or provider services change.
Hardware execution requires user-owned provider accounts, credentials, queue time, and possible usage costs.
It does not validate scientific correctness for every domain model or Hamiltonian.

use_cases.title: Prototype Quantum Circuits
use_cases.description: Create Bell states, QFT circuits, parameterized ansatz circuits, and measurement workflows with clear Qiskit examples.

use_cases.title: Prepare Hardware Experiments
use_cases.description: Select backends, transpile circuits, manage jobs, and compare local simulation with IBM Quantum hardware execution.

use_cases.title: Explore Quantum Algorithms
use_cases.description: Build starting points for VQE, QAOA, quantum kernels, chemistry simulations, and optimization experiments.

prompt_templates.title: Create a First Circuit
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me build a two-qubit Bell state in Qiskit, run it locally, and explain each step.

prompt_templates.title: Compare Execution Options
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my Qiskit circuit goal and recommend whether I should use StatevectorSampler, Aer, or IBM Quantum hardware.

prompt_templates.title: Optimize for a Backend
prompt_templates.prompt: Given my target backend and circuit constraints, suggest a transpilation strategy and explain the tradeoffs.

prompt_templates.title: Design an Algorithm Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me design a Qiskit workflow for VQE or QAOA, including ansatz choice, primitive selection, optimization, and result analysis.

output_examples.input: Build a beginner Bell state experiment.
output_examples.output: A step-by-step circuit plan, simulator execution path, expected measurement distribution, and explanation of entanglement.

output_examples.input: Prepare my circuit for IBM Quantum hardware.
output_examples.output: Backend selection advice, transpilation settings, queue and shot considerations, and credential handling reminders.

output_examples.input: Plan a VQE experiment for a small molecule.
output_examples.output: A workflow outline covering Hamiltonian mapping, ansatz selection, estimator usage, classical optimization, and result checks.

best_practices: Start with local simulators before submitting work to quantum hardware.
Keep provider tokens outside prompts and load them from secure local configuration.
Transpile and inspect circuit depth, gate counts, and backend compatibility before execution.
anti_patterns: Do not assume simulator results will match noisy hardware results exactly.
Do not paste real IBM Quantum tokens into shared chats or public files.
Do not run expensive hardware jobs before testing small circuits and shot counts.

faq.question: Does this skill run quantum code automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and examples that users can adapt in their own environment.

faq.question: Can it help with IBM Quantum hardware?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers account setup, backend selection, runtime primitives, sessions, jobs, and result retrieval.

faq.question: Does it require a paid account?
faq.answer: Local simulation does not require an account. Hardware execution depends on provider access and service terms.

faq.question: Which Qiskit primitives does it cover?
faq.answer: It covers sampler and estimator workflows, including local statevector primitives and IBM Runtime variants.

faq.question: Can it explain transpilation choices?
faq.answer: Yes. It describes optimization levels, backend topology, native gates, routing, and circuit depth tradeoffs.

faq.question: Is the security audit blocking publication?
faq.answer: No. Manual review found low residual risk and no confirmed malicious behavior.
